comments.title: Review of In Search of Odhinn and Question on Changes to thoughts on Theology
comments.content:
Good Evening,

May all who mark it have a happy and blessed Lughnasadh this week, and for everyone else, may your summer be coming along nicely.

To Share: I've put together my thoughts on Shani Oates's In Search of Odhinn for anyone who may be interested: https://jpowellrussell.com/#book_in_search_of_odhinn

To Ask: We talked a bit about A World Full of Gods last week, and a project I have on the backburner at the moment is to read more on (polytheistic) theology. Despite its poor sales, do you have any plans to expand on what you wrote in that book, and whether you want to put it into book form or not, has your thinking on the subject matter changed in any substantial ways since publishing that? If so, would you mind sharing a thumbnail version of how so?

As always, thanks very much to JMG and everyone else here for all that you do.

My blessings to all who welcome them,
Jeff

replies.title: Re: Review of In Search of Odhinn and Question on Changes to thoughts on Theology
replies.content: I did some revisions to the second edition, mostly to improve a couple of the philosophical arguments, but on the whole I'm satisfied with the book. If I were to do another book on similar topics it would be on the theology of polytheist worship, with a cross-cultural perspective and close attention to polytheist philosophical stances from Japan, China, India, and the classical world.

replies.title: Re: Review of In Search of Odhinn and Question on Changes to thoughts on Theology
replies.content:
Thank you! I, for one, would be extremely interested in this hypothetical book. If I may ask a clarifying follow-up question, when you say "theology of polytheist worship" would that differ from other looks at worship mainly by focusing on the understanding of what is going on in worship, rather than, say, an ethnographic description of what a ritual or prayer looks like?

Cheers,
Jeff

replies.title: Re: Review of In Search of Odhinn and Question on Changes to thoughts on Theology
replies.content: That's correct. I'm not an ethnographer, and I'll leave them to handle outside descriptions. I start from the paired recognitions that gods exist and that the point of worship is to interact with them in a way that is acceptable to them and beneficial to us.  I want to know if it's possible to generalize from the arrangements made in existing polytheist traditions of worship to develop a general theory, or a set of commonly applicable strategies, concerning how to interact with gods.

replies.title: Re: Review of In Search of Odhinn and Question on Changes to thoughts on Theology

I even saw a crocheted tlaloc been prayed at like a saint alongside the christian saint Santiago Apóstol, a rain Saint. It seems that some of the ancient gods are being reinterpreted as a manifestation of christian deity right next to polytheists that are also worshiping the gods in a more revivalist kind of way (like fruit offerings and dance rituals).

As for activity, they used to say that if you wanted Tlaloc to give you a rain free day, you had to stick a metal knife in your garden. I didn't try that but on my birthday last week I went to the roof and asked Tlaloc if he thought that my plans were according to his. I swear to Tlaloc in that moment I saw a heart shaped cloud form and disappear in a few minutes. The get together was a success and it rained the rest of the week! TSW.

replies.title: Re: Review of In Search of Odhinn and Question on Changes to thoughts on Theology
replies.content:
Sounds like this will be an awesome book!

FYI, I have read a book that attempts to do just that; The Deities are Many by Jordan Paper.
https://www.goodreads.com/book/show/1875090.The_Deities_Are_Many

The author himself is/was (not sure if he's still alive) both a practicing polytheist and an academic. His book provides a very rare perspective from that approach and he seems to provide us many valuable insights that are almost completely lacking in other sources.

During his research in Taiwan he married a Chinese wife and got to participate directly in her family's folk traditions; he talks a lot about Chinese spirit mediumship and divination practices. He also had direct experience working with Native Americans. From his interactions with those traditions he attempts to construct a general theology of polytheist worship, cross-culture.

The main weakness though I see in the book is that he conflates all western traditions into one big blob and takes a very derisive stance toward anything Western, in a sort of lefty-deconstructionist sort of way. He also seems dismissive of applying philosophical interpretations to polytheist traditions. Still though, you might find glancing at this book to be valuable as a way of seeing what other people have done to address the topic you will be writing your book about.

My gut tells me you will write a much better book on this topic ;)

replies.title: Re: Review of In Search of Odhinn and Question on Changes to thoughts on Theology
replies.content: Jordan Paper is still alive last I checked; he was born in 1938, so he's only in his mid-80s. He is a Professor emeritus of Religious Studies and of East Asian Studies at York University in Toronto. He has written a good number of scholarly books on the broad subject of spirituality and spiritual practices, some emphasizing Chinese, others American Indigenous practices (from the days before the Indigenous began to be quite vocal against non-Indigenous studying their traditions, which change he respected). He also wrote one quite good book on mystical experiences, which he himself has had at various times. His other works are quite academic. His The Deities are Many is his deliberate departure from the academic mode of writing into personal accounts. I find it every bit as insightful and valuable as our host's excellent A World Full of Gods.

replies.title: Re: Review of In Search of Odhinn and Question on Changes to thoughts on Theology
replies.content:
An interesting and worthwhile counterbalance is Michael Saso's Mystic, Shaman, Oracle, Priest, which is among other things a series of studies of Christian (specifically Catholic), Taoist, Japanese Tantric Buddhist, Korean Shamanic, and a few other traditions -- into many of which he was an initiated practitioner. The book is also something of an autobiographical memoir, which is very helpful in seeing just what he is doing, and in understanding a world that has almost vanished.

He started as a Jesuit. He studied in the Jesuit seminary near Spokane, where he was introduced to local native American practitioners and practices. He was trained in Chinese and Japanese, ordained to the priesthood, and sent to Taiwan (where he studied with Taoist priests, and became an ordained Taoist priest) and Japan, where he studied Tendai. He left the Jesuits, married, had children; his marriage was annulled, and eventually, after a long career, he was admitted back into the priesthood (with a Papal dispensation), and now. if I remember correctly, serves as a parish priest in or near San Jose. (https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Michael_Saso)

In contrast to Paper, he did not renounce any of these connections, but in the Jesuit eirenical style affirms them all. (He is very committed to interfaith dialogue.)

His basic model is this. In all the traditions he has entered, and in his understanding in religious traditions in general, there are two components, the technical names of which are apophatic and cataphatic.

The apophatic is the Path of Negation, which aims at realizing the Ground (or Unground) of Being as it is in itself, beyond all specifiable forms. This, he is convinced, is present in all the traditions he has entered, and others that he has studied, although it is often more or less secret or at least reserved, very much because it tends to cross sectarian and cultural lines.

The cataphatic is the Path of Affirmation, which embodies, or is closely tied to, specific cultural forms, whether polytheist or monotheist. It has to do with the presence and operation of divine energies manifesting in the specific world of experience, natural and cultural, of individuals and communities

From his point of view, both types of practice are appropriate. Although he doesn't use this terminology, I think it would be fair to say that he feels he has resolved the binary between religious and cultural multiplicity (and polytheism) on the one hand, and unity (and mystical monotheism) on the other.

From just about any point of view he might well be seen as gravely eccentric, but he has managed to stick to his calling, his lex privata, throughout a long and productive life. (His own website, which doesn't seem to have been updated for a while, is at https://michaelsaso.org/ .)

LeGrand

replies.title: Re: Aphantasia and occult practices

comments.title: LBRP and the tarot
comments.content:
Hey JMG

Recently, it occurred to me that if you treat the 4 archangels of the LBRP as their corresponding Sephitoth, then a tarot card, equivalent to a path, can be assigned to each space between them.
I have meditated on this for a while, and already 2 things of interest have become apparent.

1-The cards form a cross of 5 cards, one diagonal formed of cards that involve fire in some way (20,19,14) and another that corresponds to earth (15, 21). Note also the centre of this cross is occupied by 19, The sun.

2-The Card that sits between Raphael and Michael, in the southeast, is ruled by 15, the devil. It turns out that it is this exact direction that requires two crosses to be traced in the R.C. ritual, rather than the single cross sufficient in all the other 5 directions. I don't think this is a coincidence.

J.L.Mc12

replies.title: Re: LBRP and the tarot
replies.content: Hmm! A very solid meditation on a neglected subject; thank you for this.

replies.title: Re: LBRP and the tarot
replies.content:
Tarot is a major focus of my daily meditations, which I have been doing for about 5 years now. In particular, much of my meditations on the Tarot concern what I call its "Sub-plots". If you consider the entire sequence of the major and minor arcana as one long story with a single Plot, I focus on the "Sub-plot" created from parts of this bigger story that are united by some common theme or connection with much of the other symbolic structures of the Golden Dawn system, such as the sub-plot contained within the cards corresponding to the paths that connect the 4 Sephiroth of the Archangels of the LBRP. I have found many other interesting Sub-plots, but to be honest most of my study still feels shallow.
You see, most of my meditation is just noticing visual patterns within the cards, kind of like playing "Spot the difference" or "Where's Wally", there isn't much deep spiritual insight or theory in most of it yet. I like to think that as I develop, I will be able to get deeper meaning out of the sub-plots beyond just noticing that 3 cards in this 5 card sequence hold 1 object, and the rest hold 2 objects, but such deep insight seems to be a formidable challenge to develop, like trying to write a novel as deep and long as "In search of lost time" or "War and Peace", or creating a scientific work like the "Principia".

J.L.Mc12

comments.title: (no subject)
comments.content:
I found out a bit more about how those Australian Aboriginal curses work, courtesy of Tyson Yunkaporta's Sand Talk:

"It is a metaphor based on deception, and in an Aboriginal worldview this is how curses work. You take a part of a system (eg. a person's hair) and observe the pattern of the whole system (e.g. their body, mind and spirit) in that fragment, then you sing a false pattern (e.g. you are dying) into the whole from the part, powering it with another system in constant motion (e.g. running water). The curse is a deception made real-- either an outright lie or a true law or pattern applied where it doesn't belong. It is like a computer virus, a sneaky line of code that ends up crashing the whole system."

That does sound like Etheric plane magic, I think? So the red bag amulet should defend against it?

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: No, this is a very sophisticated and thoughtful way of understanding magic and it includes etheric, astral, and mental directions. A red bag will certainly help but I'd recommend a daily protective ritual in addition to that.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: That is alarming. Thank you.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: The aboriginal peoples have been doing this stuff for 40,000 years. They've had plenty of time to figure it out and get good at it. The one upside is that if you know that this is how they're doing it, and you have the magical chops, you can shred this kind of working very promptly by identifying and isolating the false pattern, cutting it loose from the system that powers it, and either earthing it out into uselessness or letting it return to its sender.

comments.title: Glastonbury
comments.content:
Hi Jmg,

I will be visiting Glastonbury for a couple of weeks in a few months. Do you have any tips on stuff to see or do there?
Also, do you (or any other) have book recommendations regarding Glastonbury?

Greetings, w

replies.title: Re: Glastonbury
replies.content:
The ruins of the Abbey, the Tor, and the Chalice Well gardens are high on the must-see list. I also like the occult bookstores in town, but I'm a book geek. You might also walk out toward Street to Pomparles Bridge. "Pomparles" is a rounded-off version of the Latin "Pons Perilis" -- yes, the Perilous Bridge, across which Arthur's knights rode to seek the Grail. (Nobody waited there to ask me questions three, ere the other side I could see, but you may be luckier!)

As for books, Patrick Benham's The Avalonians is very good; John Michell's New Light on the Ancient Mystery of Glastonbury is another classic.

replies.title: Re: Glastonbury
replies.content: Thank you!

replies.title: Re: Glastonbury
replies.content:
And while youre there visit west kennet longbarrow, near avebury, which has a stone circle in the village, preferably at night with an offering.

The wee christian community room opposite the park is quite interesting too, and I am not a Christian!

And talk to the randoms that you meet, and you never know where you might end up! Have fun!

comments.title: Grand Central Station?
comments.content:
“The true delight of the soul lies in love, harmony, and beauty, the outcome of which is wisdom, calm, and peace; the more constant they are the greater is the satisfaction of the soul.

“As man begins to see clearly through human life, the world begins to appear as a forest to him, filled with wild animals, fighting, killing and preying upon one another.

“There are four different classes of men who harmonize with each other in accordance with their different states of evolution: angelic, human, animal, and devilish.

“The angelic seeks for heaven, and the human being struggles along in the world; the man with animal propensities revels in his earthly pleasures, while the devilish man is engaged in creating mischief, thereby making a hell for himself and for others. Man after his human evolution becomes angelic, and through his development in animality arrives at the stage of devil.”

JMG - do you think the earth is like a train station with folks passing each other as they head in various directions?
A way station or grand central sort of thing.

Seems to me like the universe is giving humans a chance to pick which direction(s) they want to head in - folks get to choose whether they exist in a creative and imaginative world like this: https://x.com/buitengebieden/status/1816848114814218720 (that bacon posted over on frugal friday), or if they'd prefer a trip to Paris for the sport and other 'entertainments' or perhaps some other path.

Sometimes it makes me wonder...

replies.title: Re: Grand Central Station?
replies.content: I think that's a workable metaphor, certainly. What the world is in itself, as I see it, is beyond anything we can even begin to understand, so an assortment of good metaphors is all we can have.

replies.title: Re: Grand Central Station?
replies.content:
Increasingly I've been thinking it's a little bit like Dion Fortune's "to train the mind, not inform it" but applied in a way and on a scale we simply cannot comprehend. Fascinating, but not a little vexing and extremely curious-making.
Like it is not so much the actual metaphor that is important, but rather the purpose(s) it serves is/are key.
Almost like a distraction for the personality to give the opportunity for something to unfold!
A pattern-breaker if you will, like the disorientation that occurs in walking a labyrinth.
It is all rather marvelous...

comments.title: Sensing good energy
comments.content:
When driving around, I frequently will notice if a place has 'good energy' or 'bad energy'. My partner is less sensitive to such things. Recently we were driving down a road neither of us had been down before and I noticed very good energy. Before I said something, he remarked, Wow, this stretch of road feels good. It went on for about a mile or so. This was a rural area, just a few houses around. I saw a house in the distance and got a feeling that it was the source of the good energy.

Is it likely that someone was doing magical practices that extended that far out to the land around them, including the road. If it's possible to say, what type of magic might they be doing and if it was that big does it mean it was a powerful person? Since I was possibly detecting it, either it's not secret, which may be a risk to them or they didn't care if non-malicious people caught it. I'm counting myself as non-malicious because my main thought was 'I want to have a cup of tea and talk to this person'. Why would it not be secret?

Is there an etiquette when people who know these things accidentally run into others?

Thank you very much.

replies.title: Re: Sensing good energy
replies.content:
Might be magic, might be some other kind of positive spiritual practice. The person who lives in that house might be a devout Methodist who studies the Bible and prays for an hour every day. If it's magic, the etiquette is that you don't approach someone unless they give some sign that they're open to approach -- they may be in the middle of an intensive magical working that any interruption would disrupt.

Thus your best option is to invoke whatever gods you pray to and ask them to arrange for a meeting between you and the person who is radiating that energy. If it's meant to happen, it'll happen; if not, you've just had an extremely useful lesson in what you can accomplish if you decide to.

comments.title: curse object?
comments.content:
A couple days ago I found a couple items at the base of a large white pine in a local park--a dime, and some kind of fruit or vegetable, maybe a potato, which was rotting, with several sewing needles stuck into it. I had picked up the dime as the first object I saw, but after I saw the needles I threw the dime back on the ground. There was also half a lemon nearby--not sure if that was part of it. It seemed likely that this is some kind of spell, probably a curse. Are you familiar with this particular spell? I performed the Druid's Egg right after that, and I doubt the spell harmed me, but I wonder if there are additional steps I should take to make sure. Whatever it is, it's none of my business at best.

A second, unrelated question. I'm contemplating will and desire, and they seem very closely related to me, practically 2 sides of the same coin. When you speak of "divided will," do you mean that will and desire are at odds? Or are there actually 2 wills, or 2 parts of will?

Thanks very much.

replies.title: Re: curse object?
replies.content:
1) No, I don't know that one. You should be fine, though, since you put the dime right back.

2) They're related but not the same thing. You can desire something and not will it -- when you decide not to do something even though you want to do it, for example. You can will something and not desire it -- when you decide to do something you don't really feel like doing, for example. A divided will is in fact a split in will; desire is always divided. Consider wanting to make a lot of money, and also wanting to spend a lot of money. You can choose one or the other; if you try to do both, you won't accomplish either. (Although you can also choose to spend some and save some, if that meets your needs better.)

replies.title: Re: curse object?
replies.content:
Thank you for this wonderful answer.
The whole desire vs. will thing is wonderful fodder for productive musings/meditation.

replies.title: Re: curse object?
replies.content:
Will and desire or wish are profound and important things to meditate on—in magic, philosophy, religion and spiritual practice! In the concept of Heathen multiple souls that I pursue, all of the souls have their own forms of will and wish, but the two which express them most strongly here in Midgard are the Hugr and Mod souls. In my study guide called “Will and Wish: The Dynamism of Mod and Hugr,” here is the opening paragraph.

“Will and Wish are the two fundamental reaching-out forces of Mod and Hugr, the magnetic lines which connect these souls with domains of action in the Worlds. These concepts are so powerful in the older Germanic languages that we can find them as personifications. Odin’s brother’s name, Vili or Wiljon, means “Will”. In Grimm’s Teutonic Mythology (p. 144ff) there are several long pages of examples from German poetry and aphorisms of the 12th and 13th centuries, showing Wish (Wunsch, Wusc) as a powerful, creative, godlike being. One of Odin’s bynames in Norse is Oski or ‘wish’. Both of these words, will and wish, had broader and deeper meanings in the old Germanic languages.”

In the section on the Dynamic Tension of Will and Wish:
“I view Will and Wish as closely related, but not identical forms of energy or power. The primary difference I see is that Will or determination is a ‘push’ coming forth from inside ourself, while Wish or longing is a ‘pull’ toward something outside ourself. This difference may not matter if we are looking at these meanings in an intellectual or generalized way. They do matter, however, when we try to sense how their energies are embodied within our own being, our soul-body complex, which is how I am trying to guide much of our practical soul work here. When we combine these energies of push and pull together, going in the same direction, the sum total of the two is of course more powerful than either acting alone. If they are working at cross purposes from each other, they subtract from each others’ power, or fail to amplify it.”

It seems to me that the more we consider these phenomena of Will and Wish, the more deeply we plunge into the hidden depths of ourselves and at the same time understand others more deeply.

Winifred Hodge Rose
HeathenSoulLore.net

Oh? Do you ever really, truly only desire one thing? Most people desire a vast number of things. Right now, for example, if I examine myself, I find that I desire to keep breathing; I desire food (it's getting toward dinner time here); I'm starting to feel the first stirrings of a desire for sleep; I desire to make a certain correction in a novel I'm writing; I desire a glass of bourbon and water; I have a whole flotilla of desires regarding my late wife, and the mere fact that those cannot be fulfilled in this world don't make them go away; I desire to scratch an itch on the right side of my nose -- I could go on. Human life is an endless cascade of desires, and it's quite common for them to include two or more contradictory things at once -- for example, once I wrap up Magic Monday, I'll have to decide whether to satisfy the desire for sleep or the desire to make that correction in chapter 9 of The Pallid Mask, and so on.

That's the human condition. What you choose to do about those desires is another matter, but that's the realm of will, not of desire.

comments.title: Odd Notes on Cord Magic
comments.content:
Since I seem to not be the only one with a recent interest in this topic, I will post some work I've been doing in hopes that others might get ideas of the resources out there, or share their own experiences.

Thanks to several suggestions here, I purchased R.J. Stewart's The Spirit Cord, and while I'm still early on, it seems like a fine resource for both theory and practical magic. He asks a LOT of work, and dispels a lot of common New Age delusions about the practice, which to me is an excellent sign of quality.

I also bought The Thread-Spirit by Mark Siegeltuch, which is more of an academic collection of history, myth, and symbolism. I haven't begun reading in earnest but it's a very nice volume, there are lots of pictures, and thumbing through I already discovered several practical nuggets. For example, the Egyptians used a charm or talisman formed by tying seven overhand knots, then attaching the ends with a square knot. The square knot was also called the Hercules knot in the ancient world, and seems to have been a favorite for positive works of magic.

I have also been learning patterns from Decorative Fusion Knots by J.D. Lenzen (who you can also find on youtube as Tyingitalltogether). This is the more intricate end of the practice, and though I am far from using these knots in works, I can already see how many of them lend themselves to wonderful purposes. It also gives the knotter a much more complex understanding of patterns that can be used to make their own weaves and knots.

On a very practical note, my favorite knot channel on YT is First Class Amateur, and I also acquired a copy of The Ashley Book of Knots, a treasure trove, though sometimes hard to follow.

J. Romilly Alan has an interesting old volume on Celtic art, both Christian and Pagan. He has identified 8 basic knot forms present in illuminated drawings of knot work, and he shows them in the book and gives basic details on drawing them, but there were no names. I sat down with some cord and Ashley and worked out hat these knots were to the best of my ability. We can assume that these knots had importance to the Celts at least in practical terms and possibly beyond.

Knot 1 is the classic overhand knot.
Knot 2 is the figure-8 knot.
Knot 3 is the Carrick bend.
Knot 4 is probably the single harness bend, ABOK 1474.
Knot 5 is a bit of a mystery, because I couldn't find it Ashley. 1406 was similar but definitely different. Like 1474, it involved tying a simple half hitch around each tail with the other, but the direction of pull is different. I tried it and it feels very snug when pulled taut. Maybe it was so simple that it eluded Ashley, or it might have a critical flaw I don't see, but he did list many terrible knots and said as much.
Knot 6, the way it is drawn, appears to actually be a ring of wood or metal with a wood or metal toggle and a cord woven over it. In other words, a belt buckle. It could also be a poorly-represented marlinspike hitch with a cord or toggle in the hitch.
Knot 7 is a single half hitch tied around a standing line.
Knot 8 is hardly a knot. One rope is bent into opposite bights, and another line is woven under/over/under through the bights. It would require tension on both ends of the bighted rope to actually hold in place. Maybe this represents weaving, and the single line is the warp while the bights are the weft.

If you prefer to draw knots, George and Iain Bain are the classic resources. I got George's Celtic Art and find it incredibly hard to follow and light on instruction. It just shows stages and you have to figure out how to get from one to the other, but it does break down a lot of beautiful illuminated patterns and with some tinkering it can work, just don't expect to have your hand held. Iain, his son, might be better but I don't have a book from him yet as I prefer tying real cord.

Happy cording.

replies.title: Re: Odd Notes on Cord Magic
replies.content: Thank you very much for this.

replies.title: Re: Odd Notes on Cord Magic
replies.content:
Thank you, this is extremely helpful!

Some further resources along these lines:

1) I can attest to the usefulness of Iain Bain's book Celtic Knotwork as a practical "how to". In short, it shows you a system for using graph paper to layout a regular pattern, which you can make more interesting by adding barriers that block the otherwise regular over/under pattern. I've just used it to design and draw a border I'll be carving into a leather journal cover. One note: it's less helpful for the less regular kind of knots you find in a lot of Germanic work. For that, The Anatomy of Viking Art breaks down the elements of different styles and gives advice on how to create your own designs in those styles. comments.title: Mario und Der Zauberer - Thomas Mann
comments.content: Your posts on Wagner on Ecosophia have coincided with a renewed interest in German Nobel Laureate Thomas Mann on my part.  I have been a fan of Mann since I was 22 and am now 65.  Initially, I followed Joseph Campbell's work to Mann and eventually into a Master's Degree in Folklore and Mythology.  Mann's work is influenced by Wagner in a big way but also by Nietzsche, Goethe, Freud, Kleist, and many others.  I have found his works grounding during unstable times. The Magic Mountain, The Joseph and his Brothers tetralogy, and his retelling of the Faust legend set in Nazi Germany are all moving novels.   In 1930, Mann published "Mario und Der Zauberer," Mario and the Magician.  I lived in Asia for 12 years, 11 of those years were in Beijing.   I have seen the impact of totalitarian government and groupthink on people in China, in my own country, and all over the world, but Mario and the Magician seems to distill that into a neat little package like it could have been written last week.  The magician/hypnotist (Cipolla) shows increasing power over the audience until a humiliated young man shoots and kills him.  JMG and others, has anyone else read this story and found it eerily familiar to recent events?  I am a big fan of Occam's Razor, and feel like this is a much simpler explanation than many that are circulating.

comments.title: Q
comments.content:
1) Where do you and other people here fall on the phenomenological realism (Whitehead, Bergson and Goethe (etc)) versus subjectivist anti-realism (modern day Kastrup and Hoffman heavily derived from idealism) description of reality?

I am interested in what a spiritual cosmology has to say about these topics.

2) In the 7 plane system, is each lower plane an emanation from the higher plane, or is there any underlying substance to contain all of them (ie what is the sub-atomic substance)?

For example, a table doesn't have an etheric body so what is holding it on the physical plane (this is sort of relevant to Leibniz's argument about something non-extended and non-material has to hold anything that has extension).

Similarly, my intuition would be that this table exists whether we see it or not, so more that phenomenological realism is truer than the anti-realism in a lot of idealist philosophies.

3) In general, despite being actively spiritual I am little resistant to anti-realist takes because it creates the space for a lot of woo-woo/bad ideas.

replies.title: Re: Q
replies.content:
1) My cosmology is quite specifically Schopenhauerian, as modified by Eliphas Lévi. That puts me closer to Goethe's camp than to the anti-realists, but I'm not sure it's really well described by either label.

2a) Yes. ;-) The occult traditions I follow postulate an underlying substrate, called Cosmic root substance, of which every other mode of substance from the most spiritual to the most material is a modification. At the same time, each of the seven planes is emanated by the plane above it, as part of the Cosmic root substance that has already been modified by that plane condenses further to become the substance of the plane below it.

2b) A table does not have an independent etheric body. It has an etheric structure, because nothing material can exist without such a structure (just as nothing etheric can exist without an astral structure, nothing astral can exist without a mental structure, etc.) but that structure is not independent; it is simply a pattern present in the broader etheric body of the earth. Ether has extension in space and time, by the way. (That is to say, we experience it through the a priori categories of space and time.)

2c) Something corresponding to the table exists, but we have only the most indirect and faulty knowledge of what I suppose you'd have to call the Tisch an sich (Tisch is German for table) might be. The table as we perceive it is a representation, structured by the a priori frameworks of human consciousness. So the Tisch an sich exists independently of us but every one of its properties as we perceive them is co-created by the actual table and the observing consciousness.

3) I can sympathize with Dummett's rejection of naive realism, but he did a fine job of proving that the opposite of one bad idea is usually another bad idea. Did you know that he despised occultism? Read his books on the history of tarot cards and you'll get a hefty dose of the usual clichés.

replies.title: Re: Q
replies.content:
On 2, is something like this correct: the human mind via its representation the brain, learns to tune in to the representation of a "table" and all other objects on that spectrum, aka the physical world, but if say a child were to be blindfolded in infancy, it would simply never develop its eyes, and the specific pattern that gives rise to the representations we call physical reality.

The child would be blind and he/she wouldn't be able to develop sight later because the brain doesn't have the plasticity anymore. Spiritual sight also works somewhat like this, except its via the nervous system and its connection to the subtle bodies?

replies.title: Re: Q
replies.content: A person born blind develops representations, they're just not visual. Many people blind from birth have extremely intricate representations based on touch, hearing, and smell. People born blind sometimes get vision in later life after surgery; it then takes them a long time to learn how to assemble the chaos of visual impressions together into a representation of the world. It's quite common for such people, while they're still learning how to make sense of sight, to shut their eyes when they have to do something, so they can do it more easily without the confusing visual stimuli!

replies.title: Re: Q
replies.content:
Why is it that some properties of things (such as weight height etc) are obvious even when we haven't learnt of the object, but other things (such as flavor, gradation of color) have to be noticed through a conceptual scheme? Are they all existing a-priori in the objects / whatever is out there and we learn to notice the deeper patterns via experience?

Separately I am wondering about the objective to purely subjective perceptions of external objects (whether physical, or art, music etc). What are your thoughts?

replies.title: Re: Q
replies.content:
1) Habit. We spend our first months of life learning how to assemble the confusion of the senses into a set of representations that form a coherent world, and such things as height and weight are among the things we master first. None of them exist a priori -- all of them are co-created by the interaction between the Ding an sich and the observing consciousness.

2) An objective perception is a contradiction in terms. A perception is only a perception to a subject, and is thus subjective.

replies.title: Re: Q
replies.content: On JMG's 1) above: The need to learn this stuff becomes very clear if you spend a lot of time closely observing babies. From the mistakes they make, you can infer something of their incomplete scheme of how the world works around them. One that my almost-two-year-old is just starting to get the hang of, but still makes hilarious mistakes about: volume of containers. Relative size seems incredibly self-evident to most adults, but toddlers will try to literally get inside tiny cups, or cram a stuffed animal as big as they are into a small bag. The other day, she was also impatiently making word-like noises and gesturing at the fan on the rather tall ceiling in my dad's house - she clearly thought even if she couldn't reach it, I could, and was upset that I wasn't obliging her.

replies.title: Re: Glass bottle, picture, moving water
replies.content:
A vegetable stone is a spagyric product -- that is, something created through herbal alchemy. One of the basic spagyric processes is the separation of an herbal tincture into three components, the essential oil, the alcohol, and the mineral salts. This usually takes a whole sequence of processes. The vegetable stone does it instantly: you put a vegetable stone into the tincture, and in a matter of minutes the essential oil floats to the top and the salts sink to the bottom. Making it is a very long and demanding process.

But you're right that it's also a good band name!

replies.title: Re: Glass bottle, picture, moving water
replies.content: A specialized form of talisman. You make it according to very specific patterns and consecrate it in a major ritual -- the usual way is to use the Z-2 formula taught by the Golden Dawn. Then, instead of putting it away to do its work, you sit down in front of it every morning, leave your physical body, go into the tablet, and perform the invoking ritual of whatever energy you invoked into it (i.e., if it's a flashing tablet of the Sun, you do the Greater Invoking Hexagram Ritual of the Sun) in your astral body, while at the center of the astral structure of the talisman. This has the effect of cranking up the power of the talisman to 11. Morning after morning, you do that, amping up the power. You can accomplish astounding things with a flashing tablet, but it requires a great deal of practical skill, and you have to be exquisitely careful about ethics, because any blowback will land on you like a shipping container full of neutron stars.

replies.content: Another option beyond the self employment option would be to pick out a country, identify the trades it's desperate for and get trained in one, learn the local language and culture, and immigrate that way.
